title: Are Humanoid Robots Safe to Work Alongside?
description: How humanoid robots are made safe to work near people: sensing, safety systems, risk assessment, standards, and what responsible deployment looks like.
quickAnswer: Humanoid robots can be safe to work alongside when they are deployed responsibly — with sensing to detect people, safety systems that slow or stop the robot near humans, a proper risk assessment, and compliance with established machinery-safety standards. Safety is not a property of the robot alone; it comes from the whole system: the task, the workspace, the safeguards and the training around it. Done well, humanoids work near people much as collaborative robots already do.

body.text: Safety is a system, not a feature

The honest answer to "are humanoid robots safe?" is: it depends on how they are deployed. A capable robot in a poorly designed setup can be unsafe; a sensibly deployed one can be very safe. Safety comes from the combination of the machine, the task, the workspace and the procedures — the same principle that governs all industrial machinery.
How humanoids are made safe near people

rows: Cameras and sensors detect humans entering the robot's space.
The robot slows or pauses as people get closer, keeping a safe gap.
Movements and grip are limited so contact stays below harmful levels.
Clear, accessible stops halt the robot instantly.
Balance systems reduce the risk of falls and unexpected motion.
Tasks and routes are designed so robot and human paths are managed.

body.text: Responsible deployment in an industrial setting follows the same safety discipline as any machinery: a documented risk assessment, appropriate safeguards, and compliance with relevant standards. The robotics industry already has well-established safety standards for industrial and collaborative robots, and the principles — identify hazards, reduce risk, verify, train, review — carry directly across to humanoids.

A key idea borrowed from collaborative robotics is "speed and separation monitoring": the robot is fully aware of where people are and automatically reduces speed or stops to maintain a safe distance, rather than relying on fences alone.

items: Assess the task and workspace for hazards before anything is installed.
Design safe interaction — zones, speeds, separation and clear stops.
Start supervised, with humans monitoring and exceptions handled by people.
Train the staff who work near the robot on how it behaves and how to stop it.
Review and improve as real-world experience accumulates.

body.text: Keeping a human in the loop

runs: Early deployments deliberately keep people supervising the robot and handling anything unusual. This is good for safety and for results: it catches edge cases the robot's
has not yet mastered. Autonomy increases over time as the system proves itself, but the goal is always safe, reliable operation — not removing human judgement prematurely.

faq.question: Can a humanoid robot hurt someone?
faq.answer: Any powered machine carries risk, which is exactly why deployment includes sensing, speed and force limits, emergency stops and a documented risk assessment. With those safeguards in place and the task properly designed, humanoids can operate safely around people, as collaborative robots already do.

faq.question: What safety standards apply to humanoid robots?
faq.answer: The established machinery-safety and robot-safety standards and principles for industrial and collaborative robots apply: hazard identification, risk reduction, safeguards such as speed-and-separation monitoring, verification and training. A competent integrator builds compliance into the deployment from the start.

faq.question: Do you still need a risk assessment for a humanoid robot?
faq.answer: Yes. A documented risk assessment of the specific task and workspace is a core part of responsible deployment. Safety depends on the whole system — robot, task, environment and procedures — not on the robot's features alone.
